extra.reason: Describes the masking + destructive-prune approach that has been REVERTED out of the tree. I suppressed superseded thread ids and added a boot-time deletion instead of fixing WHY threads leak. The real root cause is now fixed in tm-7rm (missing in-flight guard let one prompt mint N thread/start calls). Closed rather than deleted: they document why that approach must not be retried — boot-time deletion keyed off a third-party SQLite file that can legitimately be unreadable.

Fixed structurally rather than by adding a seam. The two hand-written abort_shared_codex_thread_setup calls in the writer (MCP-config arm + stdin-write arm) are replaced by PendingCodexThreadSetupGuard, an RAII guard armed at slot-claim and disarmed only once the request is on the wire. The MCP-config path is now a plain '?' with no branch of its own to forget, and the release it depends on is shared with the stdin-write path — which HAS a test.

NEGATIVE-CHECKED (edit asserted landed via grep before trusting the result): deleting the guard's Drop release now fails TWO tests — the pre-existing failed_thread_setup_write_releases_the_setup_slot AND the new thread_setup_guard_releases_the_slot_unless_the_request_reached_the_wire. Control: thread_setup_response_error_releases_the_setup_slot stays green, confirming the waiter-side release is independent and the guard tests aren't passing for a shared trivial reason.

The vacuous test is deleted. Its replacement pins the guard directly: armed drop releases; disarmed drop does not; a stale guard whose setup is no longer current leaves the live setup alone.

Resolved by tm-0qr: the test that carried the unsafe std::env::set_var/remove_var was the vacuous MCP-config test, and it is deleted. The guard is tested directly against the session map with no env seam, so there is no process-global env mutation left in the thread-setup tests. Verified: zero TERMAL_DELEGATION_MCP_EXE references remain in the repo.

Fixed. codex_turn_cleanup.rs now enumerates all THREE production callers of clear_shared_codex_turn_session_state and gives each its own reason, explicitly calling out that forget_shared_codex_thread is safe by POSITION and not by its thread_id guard (thread/started can bind the thread while the setup is still pending, so {setup in flight} AND {thread bound} overlap). Also added rustdoc on forget_shared_codex_thread itself — where a future caller is actually standing — spelling out that its guard does not protect against eating a parked prompt, and that a second caller must take the setup out first.

Note tm-1ur remains open: this fixes the CALLER enumeration, not the field-level exhaustiveness guard.

Fixed. handle_shared_codex_start_turn no longer relies on the debug_assert alone: it now TAKES pending_thread_setup itself (before clear_shared_codex_turn_session_state can swallow it) and, if one was somehow live, logs 'BUG: started a turn while thread setup <id> was still in flight; the prompt parked on it has been dropped' to stderr. The debug_assert stays as the debug-build tripwire. A release build that breaks the writer-thread serialization invariant now says so instead of silently eating a user's prompt. Log emitted outside the sessions lock (see tm-zet).

Fixed. CodexThreadSetupDecision::StartSetup now carries a CodexThreadSetupRequest { approval_policy, cwd, model, resume_thread_id, sandbox_mode }, cloned inside the None => arm immediately before the command is MOVED into the slot. The StartTurn fast path (every prompt after the first) and the Parked path now clone nothing. Same motivation as before — avoid cloning a CodexPromptCommand that owns base64 image attachments — but paid for only by the one arm that uses it.

Fixed. The parked-prompt coalesce log moved out of the sessions critical section: the decision block now just returns CodexThreadSetupDecision::Parked and the eprintln! runs after the guard drops. Uniform rule now holds on this path — nothing writes to stderr while holding the shared-session lock, which every Codex event contends on. The new BUG log in handle_shared_codex_start_turn (tm-7mj) follows the same rule.
Duplicate of tm-iia (filed 2026-07-11), which already has the same root cause and a prescribed fix. Detail folded into tm-iia.

FALSE POSITIVE - not a bug. The read-only gate already denies these commands. Closing after direct verification.

WHY THE FINDING WAS WRONG
The reviewer (and my own first-pass confirmation) looked only at the metacharacter scan in claude_bash_command_is_read_only (src/claude.rs:153-162), which indeed rejects only newline/;/>/</backtick/"$(" - no bare "(". Both of us then assumed tokens.first()=="echo" would classify the segment as an allowed reader.

We both MISSED the layer that actually enforces it: the token scanner fails closed on '$' | '`' | '{' | '(' | '*' | '?' | '[' => return None (src/claude.rs:453, comment: "All rewrite the argv before execution, so classifying the literal text would be unsound. Fail closed instead."). That runs BEFORE the reader-head check, so echo (Set-Content victim.txt data) never reaches the allowlist - it is denied.

The original comment at src/claude.rs:123-129 claiming the parser "fails CLOSED on PowerShell-specific syntax (backtick escapes, $, ;, (/{ ...)" is therefore ACCURATE. My earlier assertion that it was "factually wrong" was itself wrong.

PROOF (two experiments, both against a real rebuild)
1. Added a redundant PowerShell metacharacter denylist, then EMPTIED it (neuter) -> the new nested-execution test still PASSED, i.e. something else denies. That falsified "my gate is what protects us".
2. Forced the PowerShell arm to return true unconditionally (bypassing the bash reader entirely) -> the test FAILED. That proves the test is not vacuous and cargo rebuilds.
3. Dropped '(' from the token scanner's fail-closed arm (src/claude.rs:453) -> the test FAILED. That pins the tokenizer as the actual enforcement point.
Codex's probes evidently confirmed only that PowerShell EXECUTES echo (Set-Content x y) - which is true - not that TermAl's checker ALLOWS it, which it does not.

WHAT LANDED INSTEAD (no behaviour change)
- src/tests/claude.rs: new read_only_powershell_tool_denies_nested_command_evaluation, neuter-verified against src/claude.rs:453. It pins the PowerShell-side consequence of the tokenizer's fail-closed arm: (...), @(...), { ... }, & prog, $(...), ${...}, and a nested sub-expression behind a git head are all DENIED, while plain git/echo reads still pass. This guards a real, non-obvious coupling - if a future change taught the scanner to accept '(' (e.g. to support a subshell) without re-checking the PowerShell arm, a readOnly reviewer would gain a write primitive.
- src/claude.rs: +5 comment lines recording that the (/{/$ fail-closed claim is enforced by the tokenizer, not the metacharacter scan, and pointing at the test. This is exactly what both reviewers (and I) tripped over.

The redundant PowerShell denylist I initially wrote was reverted: duplicating an invariant the tokenizer already owns would imply the tokenizer does not, and would rot.
Staged checker now derives git detection from tokenized segments, rejects quoted or escaped cross-repo git, removes hash-object from the allowlist, and adds negative regression tests.
Staged checker now accepts the intended read-only git diff and external hasher pipelines with exact-cwd guarding and regression coverage; the distinct POSIX separator defect is tracked as tm-rsz.
Staged MCP bridge hides peer tools from delegation-child tool discovery, rejects direct child invocations, and includes coverage for both paths.
Staged UI renders pending prompts from the live queue instead of a deferred value, restoring visibility during streaming turns.
Staged tail hydration logic keys eligibility and overview thresholds from total messageCount and adds the long-session demand-hydration regression coverage.

Fixed and verified: PowerShell now rejects /dev/null before the shared reader can strip it; Bash keeps the idiom. Neuter-verified both directions. Codex reviewer independently confirmed the guard satisfies the acceptance criteria, and Claude reviewer verified it is case-safe (2>/DEV/NULL is not stripped, so the '>' scan denies it). The broader 'PowerShell should not share the bash parser' concern is carried on the new cd-head bug (P1) and tm-mdx.

Fixed via option (a): PowerShell arm withdrawn ('PowerShell' => false), restoring HEAD behaviour. claude_powershell_command_is_read_only removed as dead code. read_only_powershell_tool_is_denied_wholesale pins the structural rule with every historical bypass as a case, and pins that Bash was NOT collaterally tightened. Neuter-verified: re-routing PowerShell through the bash reader fails on 'g\it status' — the guard trips on the RCE shape itself. Codex reviewer independently confirmed resolved. Option (b) (a real fail-closed PowerShell checker) is recorded on this bead if the tool is ever wanted back.

Fixed: the approval loop now tokenizes BEFORE classifying the head (['pwd'] / ['cd', _]), so no raw-text prefix can reach 'continue' ahead of the token scanner's fail-closed arm; and the cd/git guard decides 'is this a cd' from tokens, symmetrically with runs_git. Both neuter-verified. Also closed a real coverage gap this exposed: neutering the guard originally passed all 24 tests because nothing pinned a quoted 'cd' — added denied cases ('cd'/"cd"/c\d retargeting git) plus the allowed same-cwd quoted form. Codex reviewer independently confirmed resolved. NOTE: with tm-jk7 withdrawing the PowerShell arm, the exploit path is gone regardless, but the fix stands on its own — it removed a raw-text/tokenized asymmetry that could have reopened tm-cnq.

Duplicate of tm-ds1 — both describe the same defect: rearm_terminal_delegation_for_followup_locked commits Running and releases the state lock before dispatch, so a concurrent status/result poll can observe the still-idle child's stale packet and re-complete it, losing or rejecting the follow-up. Codex reviewer flagged the redundancy (tracked twice). Consolidating on tm-ds1, which carries the fix direction (transitional state / turn marker respected by refresh).

Closed per product owner 2026-07-17: no evidence the attribution work beyond the shipped label is needed.

SHIPPED and working (committed fa13d81): incoming peer messages are labelled with the sender's backend-resolved session name instead of "You" in the transcript bubble. Name resolution is backend-authoritative (the NAME cannot be spoofed).

SET ASIDE with this close (reopen only with a concrete failure - the no-evidence-no-build rule):
- Queued-peer-prompt label: PendingPromptCard shows "You" until dispatch; reconcilePendingPrompts ignores source. Cosmetic, only matters if queued-card labelling is wanted.
- Provenance-to-runtime (the "return address" idea): the backend-computed sender identity reaches the UI but not the receiving agent's runtime_prompt. I argued this was the load-bearing rail for autonomous consult/test/verify loops. That was reasoning, not evidence; the owner observed those loops working WITHOUT it. Dropped.
- Hop budget: ping-pong is the feature (consult -> test -> verify), not chatter to cap. Not needed. Dropped.
- Remote sourceSessionId forwarding (cross-machine attribution): recorded on tm-88r instead, since it belongs to the cross-machine roadmap, not this local-attribution bead.
- One residual worth a 1-line fix whenever wire.rs / wire_messages.rs is next touched: the doc comments call the attribution "unspoofable"; the NAME is, but sourceSessionId is caller-supplied on the raw route. Correct the wording (wrong security-adjacent comments are the tm-mdx trap). Not blocking.

Documented 2026-07-17 (working tree, pending commit). The ratified v2 boundary is now in the docs, replacing the stale v1 "no broad session listing" text in the two places it appeared:
- docs/features/agent-delegation-sessions.md: added a "v2 update - peer messaging ratifies a broader boundary" note after the v1 MCP-Tools boundary section. Peer tools MAY enumerate/target ROOT sessions across projects (and roadmap cross-machine); delegation CHILDREN stay unreachable; both tools filter parentDelegationId == null and reject self-targeting on id and name refs; that root-only/non-self filter is what the guard test pins.
- docs/architecture.md: corrected the "Delegation MCP bridge" paragraph that claimed the bridge "does not expose broad session/delegation listing" - now states delegation tools stay parent-scoped but termal_send_to_session / termal_list_sessions reach root sessions across projects.
Code was already right (that was the ratify decision); this closes the doc/code disagreement.

Documented 2026-07-17 (working tree, pending commit).
docs/architecture.md endpoint table:
- added POST /api/sessions/{id}/delegations/{delegation_id}/followup (re-arm terminal delegation -> DelegationStatusResponse; 409 for Running/canceled/removed-child; 404 for unknown/wrong-parent). The 200-vs-202 + JSON-rejection-envelope alignment stays tracked separately in tm-8hh.
- annotated POST /api/sessions/{id}/messages with the optional sourceSessionId field (peer attribution, backend-resolved name).
docs/features/agent-delegation-sessions.md:
- added termal_followup_session, termal_send_to_session, termal_list_sessions to the tool-name list and the signature list with accurate return shapes, plus an explanatory paragraph.
- added a "Status - what shipped diverges from this proposal" banner under Peer Session Connections marking the connection-object / hop-budget / Provenance-Is-Mandatory / termal_reply_to_session / /connect-sessions design as PROPOSED and NOT implemented, so the doc no longer asserts a mandatory provenance header the shipped fire-and-forget tool does not have.
Implemented successful command cards collapsed by default with expand control, tests, and UI styling.
Fixed search-forced command detail controls, removed the unused collapsed class, and added search plus running-to-success regression coverage.
Fixed search-forced command detail controls, removed the unused collapsed class, and added search plus running-to-success regression coverage.
Fixed search-forced command detail controls, removed the unused collapsed class, and added search plus running-to-success regression coverage.

Codex discovery now filters native subagent threads before the row cap, prunes previously imported empty top-level ghosts durably on restart, and preserves real parent/delegation sessions; live API shows zero 'do git pull' dummy cards.

Cross-session delivery now wraps the agent runtime prompt with authoritative sender name/id, a cross-repository context warning, and exact termal_send_to_session reply instructions while preserving the original visible transcript text. Peer and MCP regression tests pass.

Cross-session runtime prompts now include authoritative sender name, session id, and source repository/workdir; warn that repository instructions, Beads, and skills may differ; and provide exact termal_send_to_session reply instructions while keeping the visible transcript unchanged. Rust peer/MCP tests and TypeScript checking pass.

Cross-session runtime prompts now identify the sender only by authoritative session name/id, warn that repository instructions/Beads/skills may differ, and provide exact termal_send_to_session reply instructions. Tests use different workdirs and assert neither path appears in the envelope.

PendingPromptCard now renders the authoritative peer source session name while ordinary queued prompts retain the 'You' label. Live state confirmed the source metadata was persisted; focused UI tests and production build pass.
Made both source-aware subagent predicates NULL-safe with coalesce(source, ''), preserving legitimate NULL-source top-level threads while still excluding subagents.
Added regression coverage for NULL-source top-level threads in both source+thread_source and source-only Codex schemas; all 14 Codex discovery tests pass.
Updated the state_boot module inventory to five helpers and documented native-subagent ghost pruning.
